coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of photothermal responsive dual-chamber microcapsules for self-healing epoxy anticorrosion coatings
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Naipin
Wang, Fang
Wang, Jiajun
Liu, Jiaqi
Zhu, Xinbao
coredata.dc:description: It has been urgent that timely repair of microdamage generated during long-term service of metal insulation coatings. Hereby, we developed a novel self-healing dual-chamber microcapsule with particle size about 5 μm, in which furfuryl amine modified polystyrene-acrylic acid (PSAF) as shell material and photosensitizer Carbon dots (CDs) as Pickering emulsifier. The synthesized materials were characterized analytically, FTIR and DSC demonstrated the simultaneously existence of healing agent and curing agent in the resultant dual-chamber microcapsule. Experiments also showed microcapsules exhibited satisfying storage stability more than 20 d at room temperature as well as thermogenesis effect that allowing temperature of epoxy composite materials raised to 50 °C in 60s and fractures to be effectively minimized in time. The scratch tensile measurement results demonstrated that epoxy resin composites incorporated with 2 wt% microcapsules exhibited the optimal strength self-healing efficiency, modulus, toughness and anti-fatigue performance. Furthermore, surface characterization and EIS analysis also confirmed the long-lasting healing effect of EP/MC2% complex coating with outstanding corrosion resistance. Therefore, the photothermal responsive dual-chamber microcapsule designed in this work has application as self-healing material especially epoxy anti-corrosion coating.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-chamber microcapsule
Self-healing epoxy coating
Photothermal responsive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of microcapsules curing agent and dual-chamber microcapsules
Self-healing and mechanical properties of epoxy composites

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Inlet and Exhaust system Technology
Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Research of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (21KJA430007) and Key Laboratory of Inlet and Exhaust system Technology (CEPE2020007).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of (a) styrene, acrylic acid and PSA, and (b) furfural amine, PSA and PSAF.

FTIR spectra of (a) microcapsules curing agent, DETA, PSAF epoxy and dual-chamber microcapsules; DSC curves (b) of microcapsules curing agent and E51, dual-chamber microcapsules and epoxy; SEM images (c, d) of microcapsules curing agent and dual-chamber microcapsules.

(a) The tensile stress, (b) the tensile curves, (c) the elastic modulus and toughness and (d) the flexural strength of epoxy resin with different contents of dual-chamber microcapsules; SEM images of the coatings containing dual-chamber microcapsules before self-healing (e) and after self-healing (f).
Tensile stress vs. number of cycles to failure (S–N) for EP/MC0%, EP/MC1%, EP/MC2% and EP/MC3% specimens.
Anti-corrosion photos of carbon steel sheets with epoxy composite coatings of different microcapsules contents.
The EIS spectra (a–e) of epoxy coatings with different dual-chamber microcapsules after immersing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution from 0 to 96 h; (f) change trend of |Z|f = 0.1 Hz value of the coatings.
The EIS spectra of epoxy coatings with different content of dual-chamber microcapsules (a–d) 1–4 % after immersing in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 10 d.

(a)The surface temperatures of epoxy composite coatings upon exposure to NIR laser for 120 s; (b) The compared experiment with or without NIR laser on EP/MC2% coating after 24 h corrosion; (c) and (d) self-healing and photothermal behaviors of epoxy and EP/MC2% coatings under NIR irradiation.
(a) Photos of the dual-chamber microcapsules and DETA mixed with epoxy resin at room temperature for different time; (b) DSC curves of dual-chamber microcapsules for different time.
The schematic diagram of Dual-chamber microcapsule preparation.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of photothermal responsive dual-chamber microcapsules for self-healing epoxy anticorrosion coatings

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing Forestry Univ, Jiangsu Coinnovat Ctr Efficient Proc & Utilizat Fo, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Coinnovat Ctr Efficient Proc & Utilizat Fo

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanjing Forestry Univ, Jiangsu Coinnovat Ctr Efficient Proc & Utilizat Fo, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing Forestry University, College of Chemical Engineering,Nanjing, 210037, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, Peoples R China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Inlet and Exhaust system Technology, Ministry of Education, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Inlet and Exhaust system Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Lab for the Chemistry and Utilization of Agricultural and Forest Biomass, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, Peoples R China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It has been urgent that timely repair of microdamage generated during long-term service of metal insulation coatings. Hereby, we developed a novel self-healing dual-chamber microcapsule with particle size about 5 μm, in which furfuryl amine modified polystyrene-acrylic acid (PSAF) as shell material and photosensitizer Carbon dots (CDs) as Pickering emulsifier. The synthesized materials were characterized analytically, FTIR and DSC demonstrated the simultaneously existence of healing agent and curing agent in the resultant dual-chamber microcapsule. Experiments also showed microcapsules exhibited satisfying storage stability more than 20 d at room temperature as well as thermogenesis effect that allowing temperature of epoxy composite materials raised to 50 °C in 60s and fractures to be effectively minimized in time. The scratch tensile measurement results demonstrated that epoxy resin composites incorporated with 2 wt% microcapsules exhibited the optimal strength self-healing efficiency, modulus, toughness and anti-fatigue performance. Furthermore, surface characterization and EIS analysis also confirmed the long-lasting healing effect of EP/MC2% complex coating with outstanding corrosion resistance. Therefore, the photothermal responsive dual-chamber microcapsule designed in this work has application as self-healing material especially epoxy anti-corrosion coating.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal and alloy materials have been widely used in industry aspect, however, the problem of metal corrosion has limited their usage, from material loss and leakage frequently causes pollution and accidents [1]. Organic coatings have been demonstrated to be an effective method of preventing metal corrosion. Because of their exceptional wear resistance, bonding strength and chemical stability, epoxy resins have vital applications as anti-corrosion coatings [2]. Nevertheless, the epoxy coatings inevitably suffer from microdamage during application, and then corrosion agents will readily access the metal matrix and induce corrosion, substantially diminishing the coating's effectiveness [3–5].

In recent years, researchers have taken the initiative to give coating the capacity of self-healing ability, so that it may actively prevent the onset of corrosion and increase the durability of metal materials [6–8]. In general, self-healing materials can be roughly divided into intrinsic methods based on reversible bonds in the matrix itself [9,10]and extrinsic methods based on healing agents stored in embedded containers, such as microcapsules [11–13], hollow fibers [14], and microvascular [15–17]. Microencapsulation is a controlled release technique which encapsulates active substances with polymer materials using physical or chemical method to generate microscopic particles ranging from 1 to 1000 μm. Conventionally, researchers coated the healing agent and curing agent separately and dispersed them in resin matrix. When matrix was damaged, the healing agent and curing agent flowed out and reacted with each other to fill the cracks [18,19]. However, the problem of uneven dispersion with two kinds microcapsules existed in the matrix may probably result in a great discount in the healing effect.

To address the aforementioned issue, a strategy of combining catalyst and healing agent simultaneously in a single microcapsule has been presented. For example, Wu et al. [13] prepared a novel dual-chamber microcapsule loaded with healing agent IPDI and SiO2 -encapsulated curing agent (PEA) through Pickering emulsion photopolymerization method. The result showed epoxy composites incorporated with 15 % microcapsule had 85 % self-healing efficiency, as well as good anti-corrosion performance for salt solution immersion. There are many common approaches for constructing microcapsules, including interfacial polymerization [20], emulsion polymerization [21], in situ polymerization [19], solvent evaporation method [22], etc. Among them, Pickering emulsion polymerization method using solid nanoparticles instead of traditional surfactants, substantially boosting energy consumption efficiency, has been manifested an effective strategy for preparing microcapsules [23–25]. In addition, the high adhesion energy of Pickering particles can minimize the potential of emulsion droplet aggregation while increasing emulsion stability. Considering high temperature is beneficial for epoxy resin curing, photothermal responsive nanoparticles as emulsifier has promising application for reducing self-healing time [26–28]. Ma [29] fabricated TiN@mesoporous SiO2 core–shell nanocontainers wrapped benzotriazole as shape memory epoxy coating corrosion inhibitor, which exhibited excellent self-healing ability after 30s of NIR illumination owing to the photothermal property of TiN.

Herein, Pickering emulsion polymerization method was used to fabricate a novel dual-chamber microcapsule with carbon dots as the emulsifier. In our strategy, epoxy resin was encapsulated in the microcapsule as core material while curing agent was individual wrapped as part of shell materials. As the damage expands to microcapsule's shell, the healing and curative agents will be incorporated together to address microcracks. The synthesized microcapsules were characterized and the self-healing and anticorrosion performances embedded in epoxy resin coating was investigated analytically. Importantly, the presence of CDs in microcapsules provides the complex coating photothermal ability, representing a strategy for developing fast self-healing materials.

p-Phenylenediamine, styrene, tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethyl ether, n-hexane, trichloromethane, N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were both purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Acrylic acid, 4,4’ -azobis (4-cyanopentonic acid), epoxy resin E51, diethylenetriamine (DETA) were provided by Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. 2-Furfurylamine was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used throughout the study. All materials were used as received.

Poly(styrene)-b-Poly(acrylic acid) copolymer (PSA) was prepared as previous report [30]. Typically, 6.24 g styrene, 1.44 g acrylic acid and 0.56 g 4,4’ -azobis (4-cyanovaleric acid) initiators were mixed in 10 ml THF and reacted in a nitrogen atmosphere at 70 °C for 24 h. After reaction, the product was precipitated using n-hexane as a nonsolvent phase. The unreacted residual monomer was removed to purify the polymer followed by drying the product in a vacuum dryer.

Then, 2-furfurylamine was used to modify PAS as follows. 1.28 g PSA was mixed with 10 mL dry DMF to obtain a uniform solution. 0.323 g 2-furfurylamine was added to the solution and stirred in a nitrogen atmosphere. The reaction was carried out at 60 °C for 7 h, and resulting polymer named PSAF was precipitated into ether and dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h.

Pickering emulsion method was used to prepare microcapsule curing agent and dual-chamber microcapsule. Firstly, Carbon dots (CDs) as emulsifier was prepared by hydrothermal method [31]. Then, epoxy curing agent DETA was loaded in PSAF microcapsule as follows. 1 ml DETA and 100 mg CDs were dissolved in 8 ml water as the water phase. 1.5 g PSAF was dissolved in trichloromethane as the oil phase. The water phase and the oil phase were mixed at a high speed at 5000 rpm for 2 min, and then CDs and PSAF reacted 3 h at 60 °C. The resultant product was centrifuged and purified with deionized water and freeze dried to obtain microcapsule curing agent encapsulating DETA.

Further, 40 mg microcapsules curing agent and 20 mg CDs were dispersed in 16 ml water as the water phase, and 0.5 g PSAF and 0.8 g E51 were dissolved in 8 ml trichloromethane as the oil phase. Dual-chamber microcapsules containing curing agent and healing agent were prepared according to the above method. The synthesis process was illustrated in Scheme 1 .

Epoxy composites were created by inserting varying concentrations of dual-chamber microcapsules into epoxy groups. First, different ratios of dual-chamber microcapsules were added to epoxy resin E−51, where the curing agent was DETA, with a dosage of 1/10 of epoxy resin E51. Before manufacturing the epoxy composite coating, the surface of a 50 × 50 × 1 mm Q235B steel plate was ultrasonically cleaned with deionized water and ethanol to remove dust and oil. With a 100 μm coating rod, the epoxy mixture was then applied to one side of each dry steel. All samples were dried in a drying oven at 50 °C for 1.5 h before being removed. The control sample employed epoxy resin E51 with no microcapsules and the same thickness.

The chemical structures of the curing agent microcapsule and the dual-chamber microcapsule were characterized using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR/PerkinElmer 360). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM/Regulus 8100) was used to investing at the morphologies of microcapsules. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC/NETZSCH 200 F3) was performed over the temperature range from 30 to 200 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1 under a steady flow of ultrahigh-purity nitrogen purge and an empty aluminum pan as the reference. The extraction method was used to determine the hardener content of microcapsule curing agent, in which the extraction solvent was ethanol. The E51 content in dual-chamber microcapsules was determined by extracting the microcapsules in a Soxhlet extractor for 20 h using ethyl acetate as the extraction solvent [13,32].

Tensile tests are used to evaluate the mechanical characteristics of self-healing specimens as well as their healing effectiveness with electronic universal testing machine (LD22). The prepared sample was rectangular (80 × 10 × 2 mm) and the testing speed was 100 mm/min. As the contrastive sample, it was scratched with a No. 11 scalpel which the scratch was horizontal and mended at 50 °C for 1 h before tensile texting. The sample's healing efficiency was determined according the following equation.
H e a l i n g
efficiency
( % )
=
S t r e s
s h
S t r e s
s o
× 100 %
where Stress
h and Stress
o were stress of the sample after healing and the original sample, respectively.

The fatigue test is performed utilizing the identical machine employed for the tensile test. The experiment was carried out at three constant loads based on the ultimate tensile strength of epoxy resin, with a selected frequency of 8 Hz, and three plates were tested at each load level. To establish an S–N curve, the test continues until either failure (complete fracture) of the specimen or termination after 1,000,000 cycles, whichever occurs first. Four composite samples (EP/MC0%, EP/MC1%, EP/MC2%, EP/MC3%) are subjected to testing at equivalent stress levels. All tests are performed at a temperature of 25 °C.

On a three-electrode CHI760E electrochemical workstation, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of scratched and repaired coatings submerged in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution were conducted. The applied voltage disturbance is 20 mV and the frequency was from 105 to 10−1 Hz at open circuit potential (OCP). The working electrode is the coating, the counter electrode is the platinum foil, and the reference electrode is Ag/AgCl. Cross shaped scratches were produced on the surface of each sample and submerged in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution to test the coating's ability to resist corrosion.

An 808 nm near-infrared laser (3 W/cm2) was utilized as the light source to illuminate the coated surface to quantify the sample's photothermal properties. A mobile thermograph (HIKMICRO/H21pro) was implemented to record the temperature change.

Reversible addition fragmentation chain transfer (RAFT) polymerization method was used to synthesis polystyrene-acrylic acid (PSA) copolymer, which was subsequently functionalized with furfuryl amine and obtained polymer PSAF. Thus, the conjugated double bonds in the furan ring could then undergo DA reaction with double bonds in carbon dots to form the shell material. FT-IR was used to identify the structure of PSA and PSAF, as show in Fig. 1 . Fig. 1a showed the vibration peak of the benzene ring in styrene at 1494 cm−1, the distinctive peak of the carboxyl group in acrylic acid at 1694 cm−1 and the characteristic peak of the -C-H in styrene at 2937 cm−1 in PSA, demonstrating that the polymerization of styrene and acrylic acid was efficient [33]. From PSAF spectra of Fig. 1b, the characteristic peak of carboxyl group at 1694 cm−1 disappeared, while the characteristic peak of the amide bond at 1727,1640 cm−1, and ether bond at 1077 cm−1 appeared [30], indicating that the reaction of furfural amine and PSA was successful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of microcapsules curing agent and dual-chamber microcapsules
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, microcapsules curing agent was synthesis using DETA as core and PSAF as shell material. Then dual-chamber microcapsule incorporated with microcapsules curing agent was prepared using epoxy E51 as healing resin. From FT-IR spectra of Fig. 2 a, microcapsules curing agent exhibited -N-H characteristic peak of primary amine at 3277 cm−1, which was due to existence of diethylenetriamine. At the same time, the characteristic peak of CO in the amide group of 1669 cm−1, and the characteristic peak of benzene ring of 1500 cm−1 implied that PSAF also included in microcapsules curing agent. Moreover, -N-H characteristic peak from DETA was still present in dual-chamber microcapsule, and -C-O-C- stretching vibration characteristic peak of epoxy group at 1181,910 cm−1 [11,34], which indicated that epoxy E51 was successfully encapsulated in the microcapsule. It is well known that DSC was usually characterized thermal effect of epoxy curing process.

As shown in Fig. 2b, the curve of microcapsule curing agent combined with epoxy resin E51 revealed a clear exothermic peak at around 105 °C, indicating the curing agent DETA was properly coated in the microcapsule. On the other hand, the result of dual-chamber microcapsule demonstrated an exothermic peak at about 125 °C, with relatively backward temperature compared to that of E51/microcapsules curing agent system. The reason maybe the shell of dual-chamber microcapsule was thicker and in consequence required a higher temperature to destroy the shell material. Furthermore, morphology of the microcapsules was characterized by SEM. The microcapsules curing agent of Fig. 2c showed obvious spherical shape with relatively smooth surface, and the particle size was about 1 μm. By extraction process [17], the core content of microcapsule curing agent was 38.2 %. It can be seen from Fig. 2d that the dual-chamber microcapsule also displayed an evident spherical surface with a rough surface, which was ascribed to microcapsule curing agents implanted in the shell, and the particle size of dual-chamber microcapsules was about 5 μm. With the method of Soxhlet extractor extraction [19], the content of E51 resin in dual-chamber microcapsules was 28.7 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Self-healing and mechanical properties of epoxy composites
ce:section.ce:para: As we known that epoxy resin is fragile and microcracks might occur during stretching or application, resulting in a reduction of the useful life. Therefore, there are crucial potential applications for the self-healing epoxy resin matrix design. For epoxy/microcapsule self-healing composites, the encapsulated healing components released and filled in the cracks as the materials damaged, which can sustain the specimen's tensile stress to a certain extent during the stretching process. Thus, tensile method was used to evaluate the self-healing efficiency of our prepared dual-chamber microcapsules incorporated in epoxy composites [13]. The higher degree of tensile stress compared with the intact sample, the better self-healing efficiency of the scratched epoxy resin sample.

As shown in Fig. 3 a, the tensile strength of blank sample (0 wt% microcapsule) decreased significantly from 71.07 MPa to 38.41 MPa, comparing with the sample with a scratch. As 1 wt% microcapsule blended into the resin (EP/MC1%), strength of the healed sample could obtain 60.58 % of initial tensile stress, which was greater than pure epoxy resin (54.04 %), indicating the self-healing ability of dual-chamber microcapsule. Further, the self-healing efficiency gradually increased with the microcapsule amount from 1 % to 4 %. However, the strength of composite resin with more microcapsules could be apparently declined especially excessed 2 % addition, which may be due to the microcapsule's agglomeration caused stress concentration.

Fig. 3b displayed the tensile curves of cured epoxy samples containing 0–4 wt% dual-chamber microcapsules, and then their Young's modulus and toughness could be calculated from the stress-strain curve, as shown in Fig. 3c. The results exhibited that modulus of epoxy resin composites had no evident changes with the addition of microcapsule, but the toughness decreased sharply in EP/MC3% and EP/MC4%. This was because the weak interfacial adhesion between PSAF shell and epoxy resin that leading microcapsule surface slipped from the matrix. Therefore, EP/MC2% was the optimal composite with 73.4 % strength self-healing efficiency, 462.2 MPa modulus and 4048.7 kJ/m3 toughness [35].

According to the results of the bending test in Fig. 3d, the strength would decrease with the addition of dual-chamber microcapsules from 1 wt% to 4 wt%, indicating they did not had good compatibility with epoxy resin matrix and generated the formation of defects. However, the decline of composites strength with low microcapsule contents was not substantially, and the value of EP/MC2% was slightly higher than EP/MC1%, which was consistent with the tensile strength.

In Fig. 3e–f, the two images illustrated fractures development before and after scratch healing of EP/MC2% composite material. Fig. 3e showed that when the scratch was originally produced, the crack width was around 70 μm and had a noticeable depth. After scratch healing, the fracture was decreased to 20 μm with visible material filling in microcrack. The results demonstrated that the curing and healing agents are successfully encapsulated in the dual-chamber microcapsules and could well repaired the scratches on the cured resin.

For the fatigue tests, EP/MC0%, EP/MC1%, EP/MC2% and EP/MC3% samples were prepared and tested under different loads—300, 340 and 400 N [36]. The stress–life (S–N) curves were shown in Fig. 4 . When the strength was above 17 MPa, the fatigue life of EP/MC1% and EP/MC2% were obviously higher than that of neat epoxy resin under the same load. For instance, the fatigue life of EP/MC1% and EP/MC2% under stress amplitude of 21 MPa were 4838 and 14200 cycles, which were 4 and 12 times of EP/MC0%. On the other hand, the number of failure cycles of EP/MC1% was greater than pure EP or EP/MC2% as stress was less than 17 MPa, which might be the damage to the materials was less under low loads. The fatigue life of EP/MC3% was obviously lower than that of pure epoxy resin with the same loads, indicating more microcapsule contents would result in excessive stress concentration.

To demonstrate the self-healing performance of epoxy resin/microcapsules composites more directly, the epoxy E51 coating containing microcapsules was formed on the steel surface and the anti-corrosion capabilities were investigated. EIS testing was used to assess the coating's corrosion resistance. All coating samples were scratched and healed for 1 h before being submerged in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, and corrosion test results during 96 h are shown in Fig. 5 . From the photos of Fig. 5, the scratch of the pure epoxy coating began to show red rust after soaking for 6h, and the red rust became more and more obvious with time increasing. Importantly, the epoxy coating began to break off from the steel surface after 96 h saline immersion. When 1 wt% dual-chamber microcapsule was incorporated in the resin, rust was only found around the scratches after submerging in salt solution for 30 h, while as the content was 2, 3, 4 wt%, no obvious corrosion was found on the coating surface, indicating that the healing effect of epoxy coating became better with increasing content of dual-chamber microcapsule.

In addition, EIS measurements were carried out to evaluate the anti-corrosion performance of coatings. Among the low frequency (LF), |Z| f = 0.1 Hz parameter is frequently used to characterize the coating's protective characteristics [37]. From Fig. 6 a during the corrosion test of 96 h, the resistance value of pure epoxy coating continued to decrease with time increasing, which was because the cracks could not be repaired and then saline contacted with the substrate surface, resulting in corrosion appeared on the coating. By comparison, the complex epoxy coatings resistance changes were different with pure epoxy coating. In Fig. 6b-f, the curve showed that as the concentration of microcapsules increases, so did the increase in corrosion resistance. According to the results of Fig. 6f, after 96 h of saline immersion, the resistance of EP/MC3% and EP/MC4% coatings had hardly decreased, indicating that cracks were almost completely healed. Further, the log|Z|f = 0.1Hz value of EP/MC2% coatings decreased by 8.3 %, compared to that of pure epoxy coating declined 32 %, which showed the complex coating with 2 % microcapsule content already possess good anti-corrosion performance.

Furthermore, long-term corrosion test of the composite epoxy coating supplemented with dual-chamber microcapsules was carried out, and the results are displayed in Fig. 7 . The log resistance value of EP/MC1% coating was about 5.0 at 240 h, fallen by 31.46 % of original value. However, the log|Z|f = 0.1Hz value of EP/MC2% were about 7.0 and EP/MC3%, EP/MC4% were maintained at a high level above 7.3 during 240 h, declined by 8.56 %, 5.11 % and 6.97 %, respectively. The results displayed the resistance of epoxy composite coating decreased very slowly when the microcapsule content reached 2 %, which implied that the scratched coating was almost healed and effectively prevented corrosion from occurring.

Carbon dots (CDs) were usually used to convert absorbed light energy into heat or to release acceptor photons, resulting in a successful photothermal transition [38]. Thus, it is beneficial for reducing fracture healing time with photothermal responsive microcapsule loaded CDs. The photothermal property of the composite coatings with varying amounts of dual-chamber microcapsules were examined using infrared light for 120 s. From the result of Fig. 8 a, the surface temperature could rapidly increase under laser irradiation firstly and then achieved equilibrium. Importantly, as the microcapsule content was more than 1 %, the increasing temperature was significantly positive than that of pure epoxy coating, and EP/MC2% complex coating could raise by 25 °C. In the following, the comparison experiment with or without NIR light was carried out on EP/MC2% coating containing two scratches of about 5 mm (Fig. 8b1, b2). The top scratch was irradiated by infrared light for 1min, while the bottom one was not irradiated, and then they were put into 3.5 wt% salt solution at the same time. After 24 h, the photos displayed that the scratch without NIR irradiation was obvious corrosion while the top one is good, which is due to the damaged coating self-healed to protect the surface steel. Further, observing with an optical microscope shown in Fig. 8 c, d, the scratches in pure epoxy coating did not change after 1 min NIR irradiation with temperature of 30.8 °C, whereas the scratches in EP/MC2% coating were significantly reduced to about 2 μm from 50 μm with high temperature of 49.3 °C. The photothermal performance in epoxy composite coating demonstrateed that some microcracks could be effectively repaired through timely NIR irradiation.

Generally, the stability of microcapsules is essential for the practical application of resin composites. The dual-chamber microcapsules were mixed in epoxy E51 at room temperature, as indicated in Fig. 9 a, while curing agent diethylenetriamine was combined with epoxy E51 as the control group (the two groups contained the same amount of curing agent). The results illustrated that pure epoxy resin cured after 3 h, as the experimental group could linger in the flow condition for more than 20 d until solidifying at 30 d. Simultaneously, DSC detection of dual-chamber microcapsules shows that the cured peak value is about 125 °C (Fig. 9b), but size of the peak was significantly reduced as storage time increasing, which might owe to core material was gradually released to crosslink with epoxy resin. The results demonstrated that dual-chamber microcapsule performed well stability at room temperature.

In this work, a novel photothermal effect dual-chamber microcapsules individual wrapping epoxy resin and curing agent DETA via Pickering emulsion polymerization has been fabricated. The microcapsules were incorporated into epoxy resin coating for anti-corrosion estimation. Epoxy resin composites containing 2 wt% dual-chamber microcapsules (EP/MC2%) displayed 73.7 % self-healing efficiency after 1 h restoration and possessed improved fatigue life. Furthermore, epoxy/microcapsule complex coatings demonstrated no signs of corrosion immersing in the standard salt solution during 10 d as well as excellent storage stability at room temperature. Under NIR irradiation, the thermogenesis effects of CDs rapidly improved the surface temperature of microcapsules and triggered two interacting agents compartmentalized release to merge the coating scratch.
Naipin Chen: Data curation. Fang Wang: Conceptualization. Jiajun Wang: Supervision. Jiaqi Liu: Supervision. Xinbao Zhu: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (21KJA430007) and Key Laboratory of Inlet and Exhaust system Technology (CEPE2020007).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Materials science: share corrosion data
Nature

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.G. Li, D.W. Zhang, Z.Y. Liu, Z. Li, C.W. Du,C.F. Dong, Materials science: Share corrosion data, Nature 527(7579) (2015) 441-442, https://doi.org/10.1038/527441a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-healing and corrosion-sensing coatings based on pH-sensitive MOF-capped microcontainers for intelligent corrosion control
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Tong, D.W. Zhang, R.J. Zhang, J.K. Wang, L.W. Ma, P. Keil, A. Mol,X.G. Li, Self-healing and corrosion-sensing coatings based on pH-sensitive MOF-capped microcontainers for intelligent corrosion control, Chem. Eng. J. 454 (2023) 140335, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.140335.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Autonomous self-healing in epoxy coatings provided by high efficiency isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI) microcapsules for protection of carbon steel
Prog. Org. Coating
